content:
# Luke Chapter 16 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 108-109 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_016_page_001.jpg, LUK_016_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he said also to his disciples, "There was a certain rich man who had a steward, and this one was accused to him as squandering his possessions.

**2** And having called him, he said to him, 'What is this I hear concerning you? Render the account of your stewardship, for you are no longer able to be steward.'

**3** And the steward said within himself, 'What shall I do, since my lord is taking away the stewardship from me? I am not strong enough to dig; I am ashamed to beg.

**4** I know what I shall do, so that when I am removed from the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.'

**5** And having summoned each one of his lord's debtors, he said to the first, 'How much do you owe my lord?'

**6** And he said, 'A hundred baths of oil.' And he said to him, 'Take your written bond, and sit down quickly, and write fifty.'

**7** Then to another he said, 'And you, how much do you owe?' And he said, 'A hundred cors of wheat.' He said to him, 'Take your written bond and write eighty.'

**8** And the lord commended the steward of unrighteousness, because he had acted shrewdly; for the sons of this age are more shrewd than the sons of the light in dealing with their own generation.

**9** And I say to you, make for yourselves friends by means of the mammon of unrighteousness, so that when it fails, they may receive you into the eternal tents.

**10** He who is faithful in a very little thing is faithful also in much, and he who is unrighteous in a very little thing is unrighteous also in much.

**11** If therefore you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will entrust to you the true riches?

**12** And if you have not been faithful in that which is another's, who will give to you that which is your own?

**13** No household servant is able to serve two lords; for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will hold to one and despise the other. You are not able to serve God and mammon."

**14** And the Pharisees, being lovers of money, heard all these things, and they were sneering at him.

**15** And he said to them, "You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts; for that which is exalted among men is an abomination before God.

**16** The law and the prophets were until John; from that time the kingdom of God is proclaimed as good news, and everyone forces his way into it.

**17** But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one stroke of the law to fall.

**18** Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries one divorced from a husband commits adultery.

**19** Now there was a certain rich man, and he was clothed in purple and fine linen, feasting sumptuously every day.

**20** And a certain poor man named Lazarus had been laid at his gate, covered with sores,

**21** and longing to be fed from the things falling from the table of the rich man; but even the dogs, coming, were licking his sores.

**22** And it came to pass that the poor man died and was carried away by the angels to the bosom of Abraham. And the rich man also died and was buried.

**23** And in Hades, having lifted up his eyes, being in torments, he sees Abraham from afar and Lazarus in his bosom.

**24** And he, having cried out, said, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame.'

**25** But Abraham said, 'Child, remember that you received your good things in your life, and Lazarus likewise the evil things; but now here he is comforted, and you are in anguish.

**26** And in addition to all these things, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that those wishing to cross over from here to you are not able, nor may those from there cross over to us.'

**27** And he said, 'I ask you then, father, that you send him to my father's house,

**28** for I have five brothers, so that he may warn them, lest they also come to this place of torment.'

**29** But Abraham said, 'They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them.'

**30** And he said, 'No, father Abraham, but if someone from the dead should go to them, they will repent.'

**31** And he said to him, 'If they do not hear Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded if someone should rise from the dead.'"

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 108-109) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 15:30 through Luke 16:19 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 15:30, Lk. 16:5, Lk. 16:12, and Lk. 16:19. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 16:25 through Luke 17:18 across four columns. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΕΛΕΓΕΝ ΔΕ ΚΑΙ ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟΥΣ ΜΑΘΗΤΑΣ (16:1, "And he said also to the disciples")
- ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΣ ΤΙΣ ΗΝ ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ (16:1, "A certain man was rich")
- ΔΙΑΣΚΟΡΠΙΖΩΝ ΤΑ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΝΤΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ (16:1, "squandering his possessions")
- ΣΚΑΠΤΕΙΝ ΟΥΚ ΙΣΧΥΩ ΕΠΑΙΤΕΙΝ ΑΙΣΧΥΝΟΜΑΙ (16:3, "I am not strong enough to dig; I am ashamed to beg")
- ΕΚΑΤΟΝ ΒΑΤΟΥΣ ΕΛΑΙΟΥ (16:6, "a hundred baths of oil")
- ΕΚΑΤΟΝ ΚΟΡΟΥΣ ΣΙΤΟΥ (16:7, "a hundred cors of wheat")
- ΕΠΗΝΕΣΕΝ Ο ΚΥΡΙΟΣ ΤΟΝ ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ (16:8, "the lord commended the steward of unrighteousness")
- ΤΟΥ ΜΑΜΩΝΑ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ (16:9, "the mammon of unrighteousness")
- ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΟΙΚΕΤΗΣ ΔΥΝΑΤΑΙ ΔΥΣΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΙΣ ΔΟΥΛΕΥΕΙΝ (16:13, "No household servant is able to serve two lords")
- ΦΙΛΑΡΓΥΡΟΙ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΝΤΕΣ (16:14, "being lovers of money")
- ΕΞΕΜΥΚΤΗΡΙΖΟΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ (16:14, "were sneering at him")
- ΒΔΕΛΥΓΜΑ ΕΝΩΠΙΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ (16:15, "an abomination before God")
- ΠΑΣ ΕΙΣ ΑΥΤΗΝ ΒΙΑΖΕΤΑΙ (16:16, "everyone forces his way into it")
- ΜΙΑΝ ΚΕΡΑΙΑΝ ΤΟΥ ΝΟΜΟΥ (16:17, "one stroke of the law")
- ΠΟΡΦΥΡΑΝ ΚΑΙ ΒΥΣΣΟΝ (16:19, "purple and fine linen")
- ΠΤΩΧΟΣ ΔΕ ΤΙΣ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΙ ΛΑΖΑΡΟΣ (16:20, "a certain poor man named Lazarus")
- ΕΒΕΒΛΗΤΟ ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟΝ ΠΥΛΩΝΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ (16:20, "had been laid at his gate")
- ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΚΟΛΠΟΝ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ (16:22, "to the bosom of Abraham")
- ΕΝ ΤΩ ΑΔΗ (16:23, "in Hades")
- ΧΑΣΜΑ ΜΕΓΑ ΕΣΤΗΡΙΚΤΑΙ (16:26, "a great chasm has been fixed")
- ΜΩΥΣΕΑ ΚΑΙ ΤΟΥΣ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΑΣ (16:29, "Moses and the prophets")
- ΕΚ ΝΕΚΡΩΝ ΑΝΑΣΤΗ (16:31, "should rise from the dead")

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **16:1 — "And he said also" vs. "And he was saying also":** EMET renders "And he was saying also" (preserving imperfect ΕΛΕΓΕΝ), LOGOS "And he said also," PNEUMA "Jesus also said." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "And he said also" renders the imperfect naturally in English narrative while preserving the connective; EMET's "was saying" over-emphasizes the imperfect aspect; PNEUMA supplies the name "Jesus" which is not in the Greek.

2. **16:1 — "steward" vs. "manager":** EMET and LOGOS render "steward" (ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΣ), PNEUMA renders "manager." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "steward" preserves the formal register and its theological resonance with the concept of stewardship; "manager" (PNEUMA) is more contemporary but loses the traditional weight.

3. **16:2 — "Render" vs. "Give back":** EMET renders "Give back the account," LOGOS "Render the account," PNEUMA "Turn in a complete account." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Render" is the more precise formal equivalent of ΑΠΟΔΟΣ in the context of accounting.

4. **16:3 — "since" vs. "because":** EMET renders "because my lord," LOGOS "since my lord," PNEUMA "My master is taking away." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "since" renders the causal ΟΤΙ naturally in this context; "because" (EMET) is equivalent but "since" flows better before the quoted internal reasoning.

5. **16:3 — "I am not strong enough to dig" vs. "I am not strong-[enough] to dig":** EMET renders "I am not strong-[enough] to dig," LOGOS "I am not able to dig," PNEUMA "I am not strong enough to dig." SOPHIA renders "I am not strong enough to dig" — preserving the sense of ΙΣΧΥΩ ("to have strength") rather than LOGOS's "able" which conflates with ΔΥΝΑΜΑΙ; but without EMET's bracketed insertion.

6. **16:5 — "summoned" vs. "called-to":** EMET renders "having called-to [him] each one," LOGOS "having summoned each one," PNEUMA "called in each one." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "having summoned" renders ΠΡΟΣΚΑΛΕΣΑΜΕΝΟΣ cleanly; EMET's "called-to" is awkward.

7. **16:6 — "written bond" vs. "written-document":** EMET renders "written-document," LOGOS "written bond," PNEUMA "bill." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "written bond" renders ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΑ with appropriate formality and specificity; "bill" (PNEUMA) is too casual; "written-document" (EMET) is overly hyphenated.

8. **16:6 — "sit down quickly, and write" vs. "having sat down, quickly write":** EMET renders "having sat down, quickly write," LOGOS "sit down quickly, and write." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — coordinate imperatives are clearer in English; EMET's participial construction is over-literal.

9. **16:8 — "commended" vs. "praised":** EMET renders "praised," LOGOS "commended," PNEUMA "commended." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "commended" renders ΕΠΗΝΕΣΕΝ with the nuance of professional approval appropriate to the master-steward relationship.

10. **16:8 — "had acted shrewdly" vs. "acted shrewdly":** EMET renders "he acted shrewdly," LOGOS "he had acted shrewdly," PNEUMA "acting so shrewdly." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the pluperfect "had acted" better conveys that the shrewd action preceded the commendation.

11. **16:8 — "in dealing with their own generation" vs. "toward their own generation":** EMET renders "toward their own generation," LOGOS "in relation to their own generation," PNEUMA "in dealing with their own kind." SOPHIA renders "in dealing with their own generation" — combining PNEUMA's natural phrasing with LOGOS/EMET's retention of "generation" (ΓΕΝΕΑΝ) rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "kind."

12. **16:9 — "by means of" vs. "out of":** EMET renders "out of the mammon," LOGOS "by means of the mammon," PNEUMA "use worldly wealth." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "by means of" renders the prepositional force of ΕΚ in this instrumental context more precisely than EMET's "out of."

13. **16:10 — "He who is faithful" vs. "The [one who is] faithful":** EMET renders "The [one who is] faithful," LOGOS "He who is faithful," PNEUMA "Whoever is faithful." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "He who is faithful" is a natural formal English rendering of the substantive participle; EMET's brackets are unnecessary; PNEUMA's "Whoever" is acceptable but less precise.

14. **16:14 — "sneering at" vs. "scoffing at":** EMET renders "sneering-at" (ΕΞΕΜΥΚΤΗΡΙΖΟΝ), LOGOS "scoffing at," PNEUMA "sneering at." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "sneering at" captures the nasal contempt of ΕΞΕΜΥΚΤΗΡΙΖΩ (from ΜΥΚΤΗΡ, "nose") more vividly than "scoffing."

15. **16:15 — "for" vs. "because":** EMET renders "because the [thing which is] exalted," LOGOS "for that which is exalted," PNEUMA "What people value highly." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "for" is the standard formal causal connector, consistent with SOPHIA's established practice.

16. **16:16 — "proclaimed as good news" vs. "being-proclaimed-as-good-news":** EMET renders "is being-proclaimed-as-good-news," LOGOS "is proclaimed as good tidings," PNEUMA "is being proclaimed." SOPHIA renders "is proclaimed as good news" — combining LOGOS's formal structure with the more natural "good news" rather than "good tidings."

17. **16:16 — "forces his way" vs. "is forcing-[his-way]":** EMET renders "is forcing-[his-way]," LOGOS "forces his way," PNEUMA "is pressing into it forcefully." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "forces his way" is clean and preserves the middle voice sense of ΒΙΑΖΕΤΑΙ without EMET's brackets or PNEUMA's added adverb.

18. **16:18 — "Everyone who divorces" vs. "Everyone divorcing":** EMET renders "Everyone divorcing," LOGOS "Everyone who divorces," PNEUMA "Anyone who divorces." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Everyone who divorces" renders the participial construction naturally in English.

19. **16:19 — "Now" vs. "And":** EMET renders "And a certain man was rich," LOGOS "Now there was a certain rich man," PNEUMA "There was a rich man." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Now" marks the narrative transition to the second parable, as established in SOPHIA's practice for similar transitions (cf. 15:25).

20. **16:19 — "clothed in" vs. "clothing-himself [in]":** EMET renders "he was clothing-himself [in]," LOGOS "he was clothed in," PNEUMA "dressed in." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "clothed in" renders ΕΝΕΔΙΔΥΣΚΕΤΟ naturally without EMET's reflexive construction or PNEUMA's casual register.

21. **16:21 — "longing" vs. "desiring":** EMET renders "desiring," LOGOS "longing," PNEUMA "longing." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "longing" captures the sustained yearning of ΕΠΙΘΥΜΩΝ more effectively, consistent with the choice at 15:16.

22. **16:22 — "And it came to pass" vs. "And it came-to-be":** EMET renders "And it came-to-be," LOGOS "And it came to pass." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "it came to pass" is the standard formal rendering of ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ without EMET's hyphenation.

23. **16:23 — "in his bosom" vs. "in his bosoms":** EMET renders "in his bosoms" (plural, ΕΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΚΟΛΠΟΙΣ), LOGOS "in his bosom" (singular in English), PNEUMA "at his side." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — English idiom uses the singular "bosom" naturally; EMET's plural, while literal, is unnatural in English; PNEUMA's "at his side" loses the imagery.

24. **16:24 — "having cried out" vs. "having called-out":** EMET renders "having called-out," LOGOS "having cried out," PNEUMA "called out." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "having cried out" conveys the urgency and volume implied by ΦΩΝΗΣΑΣ in this context of anguish.

25. **16:24 — "for I am in anguish" vs. "because I am in-anguish":** EMET renders "because I am in-anguish," LOGOS "for I am in anguish." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "for" is consistent with SOPHIA's established practice for the causal conjunction; unhyphenated rendering is cleaner.

26. **16:25 — "But Abraham said" vs. "And Abraham said":** EMET renders "And Abraham said," LOGOS "But Abraham said," PNEUMA "But Abraham replied." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "But" (ΔΕ in adversative sense) marks the contrast between the rich man's plea and Abraham's refusal.

27. **16:25 — "received your good things" vs. "received-in-full your good [things]":** EMET renders "received-in-full your good [things]," LOGOS "received your good things," PNEUMA "received your good things." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "received your good things" renders ΑΠΕΛΑΒΕΣ ΤΑ ΑΓΑΘΑ ΣΟΥ naturally; the "in-full" nuance of ΑΠΟΛΑΜΒΑΝΩ is significant but belongs in notes rather than the translation text.

28. **16:28 — "warn" vs. "testify":** EMET renders "warn," LOGOS "testify," PNEUMA "warn." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "warn" captures the urgent, admonitory force of ΔΙΑΜΑΡΤΥΡΗΤΑΙ in context more effectively than the neutral "testify."

29. **16:29 — "But Abraham said" vs. "And Abraham says":** EMET renders "And Abraham says" (preserving historical present), LOGOS "But Abraham said," PNEUMA "Abraham replied." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "But Abraham said" maintains the adversative contrast and consistent past-tense narrative; the historical present in EMET, while textually accurate, creates an inconsistency in English narrative flow.

30. **16:31 — "neither will they be persuaded" vs. "neither will they be convinced":** EMET renders "neither will they be persuaded," LOGOS "neither will they be persuaded," PNEUMA "they will not be convinced." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "persuaded" is the more precise rendering of ΠΕΙΣΘΗΣΟΝΤΑΙ; "convinced" (PNEUMA) is close but lacks the interpersonal nuance of persuasion.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
